required.title: [Instructional Program]
required.author: Siegfried Engelmann, Phyllis Haddox, and Elaine Bruner

required.title: [Decodable Readers] Bob Books (Sets 1–4) by Bobby Lynn Maslen
required.author: Bobby Lynn Maslen

required.title: [Read Aloud — by the Mentor] Rich, complex picture books and stories

suggested.title: [Decodable Readers] Primary Phonics by Barbara Makar
suggested.author: Barbara Makar

supplementary.title: [Visual Resource] Writing samples from five or more scripts

competence_markers.description: The student can hear and identify individual sounds (phonemes) in spoken words — beginning, middle, and ending sounds
The student can blend three and four sounds together to read simple words (CVC and CCVC patterns)
The student knows the sounds for all 26 letters and the most common digraphs (sh, ch, th, wh, ck)
The student can read short-vowel decodable text with accuracy and growing fluency
The student recognizes at least 30–50 high-frequency sight words automatically
The student can read simple connected text (decodable books, simple trade books) and retell what happened
The student can spell simple CVC words from dictation using known letter-sound correspondences
The student knows that other writing systems exist and can name at least three (e.g., Arabic, Chinese, Cyrillic)
The student chooses to look at or read books independently, even if slowly — reading is becoming a chosen activity, not only an assigned one

narration_prompts.detail: What do the letters do? How do you figure out a word you have never seen before? Show me with a word.

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest part about learning to read?
narration_prompts.detail: What helps you when you get stuck on a word?

narration_prompts.prompt: Read me this page and then tell me what happened in the story.
narration_prompts.detail: (After reading a decodable book or simple trade book.)

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a different way that people write.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you notice about it? How is it different from English?

narration_prompts.prompt: Why do you think people invented writing?
narration_prompts.detail: What would the world be like if nobody could read or write?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me the story we read together today.
narration_prompts.detail: (After a read-aloud — the student narrates back the story they heard, building comprehension and oral expression.)

cross_connections.description: The student who is discovering that language has structure (words have jobs, word order matters) is building the conceptual foundation for understanding that writing is also a structured system — letters represent sounds in a rule-governed way, not randomly. Phonics instruction and grammar awareness reinforce each other.

Phonemic awareness — the ability to hear and manipulate individual sounds — is the bridge between 3.1.4's exploration of how languages sound and 3.1.2's work on how those sounds are written down. The student who can hear the difference between /b/ and /p/ is ready to learn that "b" and "p" represent different sounds.

Reading and writing are two sides of the same coin. Decoding (reading) is translating print to speech; encoding (writing/spelling) is translating speech to print. The student who is learning phonics is simultaneously building the foundation for spelling and writing.

The invention of writing — first in Mesopotamia around 3400 BCE — is one of the pivotal events in 1.1.1's story of early civilization. The student learning to read is personally re-enacting one of humanity's most consequential inventions.

The student who is learning to read in English is also building the visual recognition skills that will later support reading functional signs and labels in other languages and scripts (3.1.3 Weathering Storms).

Daily phonics practice requires the habit structure and sustained attention that IC1.1-A develops. The student who has learned to sit, focus, and work through a structured lesson is ready for the daily discipline that reading acquisition demands.

content.why_this_matters:
You are about to learn one of the most powerful things a human being can learn: how to read.

Reading is not natural. Nobody is born knowing how to do it. Speaking is natural — babies start babbling and talking without lessons. But reading is a technology. Human beings invented it, and every person who reads had to be taught. The marks on this page are not pictures of ideas. They are a code — a system that uses shapes to represent sounds, and sounds to build words, and words to carry meaning from one mind to another across time and space.

The code you are learning first is the English alphabetic code. It uses 26 letters to represent roughly 44 sounds. It is not a perfect code — English spelling is famously irregular, because English borrowed words from dozens of languages over centuries and kept their original spellings. But it is a learnable code, and once you crack it, you hold the key to every English book, sign, letter, label, and document ever written.

This is not a small thing. Before you learn to read, every book is a closed door. After you learn to read, every book is an open one. The person who reads well can teach themselves almost anything. The person who cannot read depends on others to tell them what the words say — and must trust that the telling is accurate.

You are also going to discover something important: the English alphabet is only one way to write. People around the world use completely different systems — different shapes, different rules, different directions on the page. Some writing systems use symbols for whole words. Some use symbols for syllables. Some, like English, use symbols for individual sounds. None of them is the "real" way to write. They are all real. They are all human inventions that solve the same problem: how to make language visible.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Phonemic awareness** — the ability to hear, identify, and manipulate individual sounds (phonemes) in spoken words, which is the single strongest predictor of early reading success
2. **Letter-sound correspondence** — knowing which sounds each letter (and common letter combination) represents, the core of the alphabetic code
3. **Blending and segmenting** — the ability to push sounds together to read words and pull words apart into sounds to spell them
4. **Decoding** — the skill of looking at printed words and translating them into spoken language, the mechanical foundation of all reading
5. **Sight word recognition** — automatic recognition of high-frequency words that appear constantly in English text, many of which do not follow regular phonics rules
6. **Early reading of connected text** — reading simple sentences and short books with comprehension and growing confidence
7. **Awareness that writing systems vary** — the foundational understanding that the Latin alphabet is one code among many
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Is Reading?

Reading is translating marks on a surface into language in your mind. That is all it is — but it is one of the most complex things the human brain does.

When someone speaks to you, the sounds go into your ears and your brain turns them into meaning. You do not have to think about it — your brain has been doing this since before you could walk. Speech comprehension is built into the human brain.

Reading is different. The human brain was not built for reading. Writing was invented only about 5,400 years ago — a blink in the history of the human species. There has not been enough time for the brain to evolve a dedicated reading system. Instead, the brain repurposes parts that evolved for other things — the visual system (which evolved to recognize faces, objects, and landscapes) and the language system (which evolved for spoken language) — and wires them together in a new way. Learning to read literally changes the physical structure of your brain.

This is why reading must be taught. Speech emerges naturally in any child exposed to language. Reading does not. Every reader in history was taught — by a parent, a teacher, a sibling, or through painful self-instruction. There is no shame in needing instruction. There is enormous power in receiving it well.

### Part 2: Sounds Come First — Phonemic Awareness

Before you can connect letters to sounds, you must be able to hear the individual sounds in words. This is called phonemic awareness, and it is the foundation of everything that follows.

English has approximately 44 distinct sounds (phonemes). The word "cat" has three sounds: /k/ /a/ /t/. The word "ship" has three sounds: /sh/ /i/ /p/. The word "straight" has five letters but only four sounds: /s/ /t/ /r/ /ay/ /t/. Letters and sounds are not the same thing — English has 26 letters but 44 sounds, which is why some sounds require two letters to write.

**What you will practice:**

- **Hearing individual sounds:** When your mentor says "dog," you say the three sounds: /d/ /o/ /g/. This is called segmenting.
- **Blending sounds into words:** When your mentor says /s/ /u/ /n/, you push the sounds together and say "sun." This is called blending.
- **Identifying beginning, middle, and ending sounds:** What sound does "fish" start with? What sound is in the middle of "bed"? What sound does "lamp" end with?
- **Rhyming:** Words that end with the same sound pattern: cat, hat, sat, mat. Rhyming games train the ear to notice sound patterns in words.
- **Sound substitution:** If you change the /k/ in "cat" to /b/, you get "bat." If you change the /a/ in "cat" to /u/, you get "cut." Playing with sounds builds the flexibility that decoding requires.

This work is done with your ears and your voice — no letters yet. Phonemic awareness is about sound, not print. A child who can segment and blend sounds orally is ready for phonics. A child who cannot will struggle with phonics no matter how many times they see the letters.

### Part 3: The Alphabetic Code — Letters and Sounds

Now the code. Each letter of the English alphabet represents one or more sounds. Learning these correspondences — which letter makes which sound — is the core of phonics.

**The consonants** (in the order most programs introduce them, starting with the most common and most distinct):

- **s** — /s/ as in "sun"
- **m** — /m/ as in "man"
- **t** — /t/ as in "top"
- **n** — /n/ as in "net"
- **p** — /p/ as in "pig"
- **b** — /b/ as in "big"
- **d** — /d/ as in "dog"
- **g** — /g/ as in "go" (hard g)
- **h** — /h/ as in "hat"
- **f** — /f/ as in "fish"
- **l** — /l/ as in "lip"
- **r** — /r/ as in "run"
- **k** — /k/ as in "kid"
- **j** — /j/ as in "jump"
- **w** — /w/ as in "wet"
- **v** — /v/ as in "van"
- **y** — /y/ as in "yes"
- **z** — /z/ as in "zip"

**The short vowels** (these are taught first because they are the most common vowel sounds in simple words):

- **a** — /a/ as in "cat"
- **e** — /e/ as in "bed"
- **i** — /i/ as in "sit"
- **o** — /o/ as in "hot"
- **u** — /u/ as in "cup"

With just these consonants and short vowels, a student can decode hundreds of simple words: sat, mat, pin, big, run, hot, bed, cup, fog, let, win, hug, and on and on. This is the first great unlock — the moment when the student realizes that the code works, that they can look at a word they have never seen before and sound it out.

**Common consonant combinations (digraphs):**

- **sh** — /sh/ as in "ship"
- **ch** — /ch/ as in "chin"
- **th** — /th/ as in "this" (voiced) and "thin" (unvoiced)
- **wh** — /wh/ as in "when"
- **ck** — /k/ as in "back"

**Long vowels and the silent e:**

- When a word ends in a consonant plus "e," the first vowel usually says its name and the "e" is silent: "cap" becomes "cape," "bit" becomes "bite," "hop" becomes "hope," "cub" becomes "cube." This is the "magic e" or "silent e" rule, and it is one of the most productive patterns in English.

**Common vowel teams:**

- **ai/ay** — /ay/ as in "rain" and "day"
- **ee/ea** — /ee/ as in "see" and "read"
- **oa/ow** — /oh/ as in "boat" and "snow"
- **oo** — /oo/ as in "moon" (also /oo/ as in "book" — same spelling, different sound)
- **ou/ow** — /ow/ as in "out" and "cow"

These patterns are taught progressively — the student masters one set before moving to the next. Phonics instruction that introduces too many patterns at once overwhelms the student. Systematic, sequential introduction is the key.

### Part 4: Blending — The Core Skill of Decoding

Blending is the act of pushing sounds together smoothly to form a word. It is the skill that turns letter-sound knowledge into actual reading.

**How blending works:**

1. The student sees the word "s-a-t."
2. They say each sound: /s/ . . . /a/ . . . /t/.
3. They say the sounds faster, pushing them together: /s-a-t/ . . . "sat!"

This seems simple, but it is the cognitive leap where many students stumble. The challenge is that individual sounds, spoken in isolation, do not sound like the word. /S/ /a/ /t/ spoken slowly sounds nothing like "sat" spoken normally. The student's brain must learn to compress the sounds together — and this requires practice, not just explanation.

**Practice techniques:**

- **Continuous blending:** Instead of stopping between sounds (/s/ . . . /a/ . . . /t/), the student holds each sound and slides into the next: "sssssaaaat" — stretching the word rather than chopping it into pieces. This makes the connection between the individual sounds and the whole word much easier to hear.
- **Word building with letter tiles:** The student physically places letter tiles to build words, then reads them. Then changes one letter: "sat" to "sit" to "sip" to "tip" to "top." Each change is one sound, and the student reads the new word by blending.
- **Decodable book reading:** The student reads books where every word uses the phonics patterns they have learned. The experience of reading a complete sentence — then a page — then a whole book — is what transforms decoding from a skill into a practice.

### Part 5: Sight Words — The Irregular Ones

English has a set of extremely common words that do not follow regular phonics patterns: "the," "said," "was," "of," "they," "come," "have," "give," "what," "who." These words appear so frequently in text — some estimates suggest the 100 most common words account for over 50% of all words in typical English text — that a reader must recognize them instantly, without sounding them out.

These are called sight words, and they are learned through repeated exposure and practice, not through phonics rules. The student sees the word, says it, writes it, reads it in context, and eventually recognizes it automatically.

**Important:** Sight words are not a replacement for phonics. They are a supplement. The student who memorizes 200 sight words but cannot decode unfamiliar words is stuck — they can only read words they have memorized. The student who has strong decoding skills and knows the common sight words can read almost anything at their level.

Common sight word lists include the Dolch list (220 words) and the Fry list (first 100 words). Your mentor will introduce these gradually alongside phonics instruction.

### Part 6: Reading Real Text — Where It All Comes Together

The purpose of phonics is reading. Not sounding out words in isolation — reading connected text with understanding.

The progression:
1. **Decodable sentences:** "The cat sat on the mat." Every word is decodable with known patterns.
2. **Decodable books:** Bob Books and similar readers that use controlled vocabulary.
3. **Simple trade books:** Dr. Seuss, Arnold Lobel's Frog and Toad, Else Holmelund Minarik's Little Bear — books with simple vocabulary but real stories.
4. **Growing independence:** The student begins choosing books and reading on their own, even if slowly.

At every stage, the mentor continues reading aloud from rich, complex books — fairy tales, mythology, science, history, poetry. The student's reading diet has two tracks: what they can decode independently (which starts simple and grows) and what they hear read aloud (which should always be richer and more complex than what they can read alone). Both tracks matter. Decoding builds the skill. Read-alouds build the knowledge, vocabulary, and love of language that make the skill worth having.

### Part 7: Writing Systems Are Not All Alike

While you are learning the English alphabetic code, here is something remarkable to know: the English alphabet is only one way that human beings write.

**Alphabets** use symbols for individual sounds. English, Russian, Greek, Arabic, and Korean all use alphabets — but they look completely different and work in slightly different ways. Arabic writes mostly consonants and leaves the reader to fill in the vowels. Korean groups its letters into syllable blocks. The same idea — symbols for sounds — takes many forms.

**Syllabaries** use symbols for whole syllables. Japanese hiragana has a symbol for "ka," another for "ki," another for "ku," and so on. Each symbol is one syllable, not one sound.

**Logographic systems** use symbols for whole words or ideas. Chinese characters work this way — each character represents a word or part of a word, not a sound. There are thousands of characters, each one learned individually.

None of these systems is better or worse than the others. They are different human solutions to the same human problem: how do you make spoken language visible so it can travel across space and survive across time?

Look at the writing samples on your wall. Notice how different they look. Arabic flows from right to left in connected curves. Chinese characters sit in neat squares, each one a little picture of meaning. Korean Hangul arranges its letters in tidy blocks. Devanagari hangs its letters from a horizontal line across the top. Each of these systems is used by hundreds of millions of people. Each is complete. Each is learnable.

You are learning to crack one code right now — the English alphabetic code. But the world is full of codes, and someday you will crack more of them.
content.formative_practices:
*Reading at Tier A1 is learned through daily, systematic practice — short sessions done consistently. The following are the core activities that structure this packet.*

### Study 1: Daily Phonics Lesson [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Follow *100 Easy Lessons* (or your chosen systematic phonics program) one lesson per day, five days per week. Each lesson takes approximately 15–20 minutes. Do not skip lessons. Do not rush ahead. The sequence is carefully designed, and each lesson builds on the last. After each lesson, the student reads the decodable story at the end.

### Study 2: Daily Decodable Reading [Ongoing — Alongside Phonics]
After completing 10–15 phonics lessons, begin decodable readers (Bob Books Set 1 or equivalent). The student reads one book per session, with the mentor listening. If the student struggles, the mentor helps them sound out the word — never supplies the word without letting the student try first. As phonics knowledge grows, the readers increase in complexity.

### Study 3: Sound Games [Early and Ongoing]
Five minutes of oral phonemic awareness games every day, especially in the first weeks. Rhyming games. "I Spy" with beginning sounds. Clapping syllables. Segmenting words into sounds. Blending sounds into words. These games can happen anywhere — in the car, at meals, during walks. They build the auditory foundation that makes phonics instruction stick.

### Study 4: Sight Word Practice [Beginning After 20–30 Phonics Lessons]
Introduce sight words gradually — three to five new words per week. Practice with flashcards, word walls, and finding the words in books. The student reads the word, spells it aloud, writes it, and uses it in a sentence. Review previously learned sight words regularly. A rotating set of index cards works well.

### Study 5: Letter Formation and Spelling [Ongoing]
As the student learns letter-sound correspondences, they also practice writing the letters — forming them correctly by hand. Spelling is encoding (writing) what phonics is for decoding (reading). The student practices spelling simple words they can read: if they can read "cat," they should practice writing "cat" from dictation. Writing and reading reinforce each other.

### Study 6: The Daily Read-Aloud [Ongoing — Non-Negotiable]
The mentor reads aloud to the student every day from rich, beautiful, complex books — picture books, chapter books, fairy tales, myths, poetry, nonfiction. This is not phonics practice. This is feeding the mind with language, story, ideas, and vocabulary that the student cannot yet access independently. The read-aloud continues even after the student can read independently. It is the single most important thing a mentor can do for a child's literacy.

### Study 7: Writing Systems Exploration [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Dedicate two or three sessions to exploring other writing systems. Look at text samples in Cyrillic, Arabic, Chinese, Devanagari, and Hangul. Discuss: how is this different from English letters? Which direction does it go? Does it use an alphabet like ours, or something different? The student writes their name in at least two other scripts (with the mentor's help). This is exploratory, not instructional — the goal is awareness and curiosity.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Phonemic Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can segment words into individual sounds, blend sounds into words, and substitute sounds to make new words. They hear the component sounds in spoken words easily and play with sounds freely.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot isolate the beginning sound of a word, struggles to blend even two sounds together, or confuses sounds that are similar (b/d, f/v, p/b).

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you doing oral phonemic awareness games daily — without print, just sound? Has the student had hearing checked recently? Ear infections and hearing issues are a common and fixable cause of phonemic awareness difficulty.

### 2. Letter-Sound Correspondence
**Developing well:** The student sees a letter and produces its sound automatically, without hesitation. When shown an unfamiliar word, their first instinct is to look at the letters and try to sound it out.

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses visually similar letters (b/d, p/q, m/w). They can name letters but not produce their sounds. They look at the mentor for help before trying to decode.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you introducing letters at the pace the program prescribes, or rushing ahead? Are you practicing the letters in isolation and in words? Has the student had vision checked? Visual tracking issues can make letter recognition difficult.

### 3. Blending
**Developing well:** The student can smoothly blend three or four sounds into a word without long pauses between sounds. They read CVC words (cat, dog, run) with growing automaticity.

**Developing slowly:** The student says each sound correctly but cannot push them together — /k/ /a/ /t/ stays three separate sounds and never becomes "cat." Or the student forgets the first sound by the time they reach the third.

**Diagnostic questions:** Try continuous blending — stretching the sounds into each other ("cccaaaat") rather than saying them in isolation. Use letter tiles and have the student slide them together as they blend the sounds. The physical movement can bridge the cognitive gap.

### 4. Connected Text Reading
**Developing well:** The student reads decodable books with accuracy, pausing to sound out new words and then continuing. They show comprehension by retelling what happened. They are beginning to read with expression rather than monotone decoding.

**Developing slowly:** The student can decode individual words but loses meaning when reading sentences — they sound out each word without connecting them into a meaningful stream. Comprehension of decoded text is poor even when oral comprehension (during read-alouds) is strong.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the text at the right level? If the student is struggling with more than one in ten words, the text is too hard. Drop back to easier decodable text where success is high. Fluency and comprehension develop from reading text that is easy enough to decode accurately.

### 5. Engagement and Motivation
**Developing well:** The student approaches reading practice willingly, shows pride in reading accomplishments, and begins to pick up books on their own. They want to read signs, labels, and environmental print.

**Developing slowly:** The student dreads reading practice, resists phonics lessons, and never picks up a book voluntarily.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are sessions too long? (Fifteen to twenty minutes is enough at this age.) Are you praising effort and progress, or creating pressure for perfection? Is the read-aloud happening? A student who hears wonderful stories has more motivation to learn to read than one who associates reading only with drill.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Abandoning Phonics Because It Seems Boring
Phonics instruction is not exciting. Sounding out "The man ran" is not thrilling. This is fine. Not everything in education needs to be entertaining. The excitement comes when the student realizes they can read — when they decode a word on a sign or the title of a book or a note from a parent. That moment makes the drill worthwhile. Your job is to get them there, not to make the journey entertaining at every step. The read-aloud provides the richness; phonics provides the skill.

### Moving Too Fast
The most common error in phonics instruction is advancing before the current material is mastered. If the student has not fully mastered short-vowel CVC words, do not introduce long vowels. If blending is still effortful, do not add digraphs. Mastery at each stage is what makes the next stage learnable. A shaky foundation produces a shaky reader.

### Neglecting Writing
Reading (decoding) and spelling (encoding) reinforce each other powerfully. The student who reads "cat" should also practice writing "cat" from dictation. When the student spells a word, they must segment it into sounds and choose the correct letter for each sound — the reverse of decoding. This bidirectional practice strengthens both skills.

### Comparing to Other Children
Reading acquisition varies enormously in its timeline. Some children decode fluently at four. Others do not click until six or seven. Both are normal. A child who is receiving systematic instruction and making steady progress — even if that progress is slower than a sibling or peer — is developing normally. Pressure and comparison create anxiety, and anxiety inhibits learning.

### Skipping the Writing Systems Exploration
The writing systems exploration at the end of the packet may seem like an optional add-on. It is not. This is the student's first encounter with the idea that literacy is not synonymous with the English alphabet — that billions of people read and write using completely different systems. This awareness prevents the linguistic provincialism that assumes English is the default. It also plants the seed for the script literacy work that 3.1.2-A2, B, and C will develop.
